summary: Graham argues that startups generally do better in the strongest startup hubs, especially Silicon Valley, because the concentration of specialized knowledge, talent, and aggressive investors creates a stronger ecosystem. While moving isn’t mandatory and practical constraints can outweigh the benefits, the default assumption should be that more intense hubs improve a startup’s odds.
key_points: Startup success depends on city-level ecosystem strength, not national identity.
Differences between hubs are matters of degree; Silicon Valley is the strongest.
Investor aggressiveness and startup literacy are higher in Silicon Valley, which drives better outcomes.
Markets for startups are centralized because face-to-face dealmaking still matters.
Exceptions exist: family, immigration hurdles, industry-specific centers, or secure local funding.
money_quotes: This is about cities, not countries.
The difference between cities is a matter of degree.
Boldness is the essence of venture investing.
The way you get big returns is not by trying to avoid losses, but by trying to ensure you get some of the big hits.
Startups are a very specialized business, as specialized as diamond cutting.
key_insight: All else equal, startups should locate in the strongest startup hub because concentrated expertise and aggressive capital materially improve their odds.
